---
title: "Biostatistics Assignment"
author: "Jolene Wium"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: false
    code-fold: false
    theme: cosmo
    df-print: paged
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
  freeze: false
jupyter: python3
editor: visual
---

```{python}
#| include: false

import os
import sys
import importlib
import math
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats

pd.set_option("display.max_columns", 50)
pd.set_option("display.float_format", lambda x: f"{x:,.2f}")

sys.path.append(os.path.abspath("../code"))

import analysis
importlib.reload(analysis)
```


## Part 1: Descriptive data analysis
### Question 1

```{python}
#| label: tbl-table1
#| tbl-cap: "Baseline characteristics of individuals (first observation per individual), stratified by sex."

df = analysis.load_data()
table1 = analysis.table1_descriptive(df)
table1.style.set_properties(
    subset=["Variable"],
    **{"text-align": "left"}
).hide(axis="index")
```

### Question 2

```{python}
#| label: fig-mean-profile
#| fig-cap: "Mean longitudinal profile of satisfaction by household workload."

# df = analysis.load_data()
analysis.plot_mean_profile(df)

```


### Question 3
Make sure you mention:

✔ “individual trajectories”
✔ “within-subject variability”
✔ “between-subject variability”
✔ “correlation of repeated measures”
✔ whether group differences are obvious or not

```{python}
#| label: fig-spaghetti
#| fig-cap: "Spaghetti Plot of Satisfaction by Premarital Cohabitation."

# df = analysis.load_data()
analysis.plot_spaghetti(df)

```


## Part 2: Generalized estimating equations (GEEs)
### Question 4
```{python}
#| label: tbl-table2
#| tbl-cap: "Comparing covariance matrixes for GEE models."


table2 = analysis.table2_gee(df)
table2.style.set_properties(
    subset=["Model"],
    **{"text-align": "left"}
).hide(axis="index")
```

### Question 5


### Question 6
*Based on your final GEE model (from Q5), fully interpret the effects of the following predictors
on marital satisfaction. For each, provide the estimated coefficient, 95% CI, and an
interpretation:
* Time
* Premarital cohabitation
* Household workload*


## Part 3: Linear mixed effects (LME) model 
*Fit an LME model to assess the association between marital satisfaction and time, including all
covariates (do not include statistical interactions terms). Fit two models with different
random-effects structures:
1. LME 1: Random intercepts only
2. LME 2: Random intercepts and random linear slopes for time*


### Question 7
```{python}
#| label: tbl-table3
#| tbl-cap: "Comparing covariance matrixes for LME models."

table3 = analysis.table3_lme(df)
table3.style.set_properties(
    subset=["Model"],
    **{"text-align": "left"}
).hide(axis="index")
```


### Question 8
*Determine the most appropriate random-effects structure and justify your choice. State which
LME (LME 1 or LME 2) is your final model.*



### Question 9
*Compare the estimated effects of the following predictors between your final GEE model (Q5)
and your final LME model (Q8):
- Time
- Premarital cohabitation
- Household workload*


## Part 4: Model validation
### Question 10
